Microprocessors and Microsystems 94 (2022) 104671

Microprocessors and Microsystems  
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/micpro  
Early RTL delay prediction using neural networks  
∗
Daniela Sánchez Lopera , Lorenzo Servadei, Sebastian Prebeck, Wolfgang Ecker  
Infineon Technologies AG, Germany  
Technical University of Munich, Germany  
A R T I C L E I N F O  
A B S T R A C T  
Keywords:  
Nowadays, the digital chip design flow starts with formal specifications, which are mapped to Register  
Transfer Level (RTL) models using different underlying (micro-) architectures. By doing so, a hardware  
designer predicts and resolves time-critical parts to achieve an RTL-design that meets all constraints after  
synthesis. However, wrong predictions can be detected only later in the design flow, thus leading to long  
design iterations. Classical methods estimating delay in early design stages are constrained to the type of  
components or are computationally expensive for larger designs. This paper proposes a machine learning-  
based approach to estimate pin-to-pin delays for RTL combinational circuits. To improve the quality of the  
predictions we combine slew and delay estimation. To that end, training data are built using features of  
components generated by a model-driven hardware generator framework. Ground truth labels for delays,  
slews, and their interdependencies are extracted using open-source tools for logic synthesis and static timing  
analysis. Two different datasets are built: one targeting logic gates and multiplexers and an enlarged one,  
which generalizes to more RTL primitives A model trained using the former dataset achieves, on average, a  
coefficient of determination ꢀ2 of 87% when evaluating over 4-bit prefix adders. Using the enlarged dataset,  
the best model reaches an ꢀ2 of 77%. On average, our models are 8.4× faster w.r.t the time required to  
run synthesis and timing analysis. Results show that generalizing to more primitives decreases the models’  
performance, but the runtime benefit is maintained. Based on the delay estimation, critical areas of the design  
can be detected and proper microarchitecture decisions can be taken earlier and faster in the design flow.  
Register Transfer Level  
Machine Learning  
Delay  
Slew  
Static Timing Analysis  
1. Introduction  
Early estimations of the delay information of a design at RTL can  
ease the design-space exploration to find the optimal implementation  
and architectural option. Smarter decisions in earlier design stages  
result in fewer iterations of the complete EDA flow, higher Quality  
of Results (QoRs), and potentially, less time-to-market. In particular,  
Hardware (HW) generator frameworks can benefit from earlier predic-  
tions, which could be used to evaluate implementation decisions during  
the generation without any human-in-the-loop.  
The chip design flow is a complex and iterative process. First,  
informal specifications are mapped to system-level descriptions. Those  
are further translated into functional, logical, and physical designs  
using Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools. Hereby, synthesis  
tools optimize the gate-level netlist to meet timing constraints while  
minimizing area and power consumption [1]. Before fabrication and  
packaging, the correct functionality of the design has to be verified,  
and the compliance of design metrics in terms of Power, Performance,  
and Area (PPA) has to be validated. Principally, designs must meet  
timing constraints and work at the desired clock frequency. Although  
transformation and optimization have been automated using EDA tools,  
it is the task of the hardware designer to map initial specifications  
to Register Transfer Level (RTL) models that intentionally meet the  
timing requirements. This mapping is challenging, as RTL models can  
be built using multiple different implementations or (micro-) architec-  
tures, whose timing behaviors are known only after costly synthesis and  
timing analysis steps.  
Classical methods estimating delay from a higher level of abstrac-  
tion use polynomial, macromodeling, and analytical approaches. They  
have several drawbacks: they are slow, constrained by the type of  
components, computationally expensive, or give only a very rough  
estimation. In the last decade, ML has proven its ability to support  
classification and prediction tasks in many fields with higher accuracy  
than classical methods. Consequently, it is being incorporated into the  
EDA flow, especially in logic synthesis, placement, and verification [2].  
This paper proposes Neural Networks (NNs) for pin-to-pin com-  
ponent delay estimation of RTL components in Application-Specific

Integrated Circuit (ASIC) designs. Extending the discussion in [3], the  
main contributions of this paper are:  
2. Background  
This section reviews background concepts related to TA in the  
digital design flow, and building blocks of an ML solution applied to  
a regression task.  
(1) We propose a solution for delay estimation using NNs from RTL  
targeting ASIC technology.  
(2) The proposed approach is reproducible for any RTL design us-  
ing different types of primitive components. In contrast to [3],  
an enlarged training dataset is built to cover logic gates and  
multiplexers, but also demultiplexers, branch, and arithmetic  
operators. The algorithm uses features from RTL and labels from  
EDA tools.  
2.1. Timing Analysis (TA)  
TA is an EDA tool to determine whether a design works properly  
at the required clock period. TA can be dynamic or static. The dy-  
namic analysis simulates the complete circuit using input vectors and  
evaluating the outputs. STA is a static approach, as it propagates the  
gate delays from primary inputs to outputs. The gate delay is the time  
needed for a signal to propagate through a gate, i.e., going from input  
to output port [4].  
(3) To improve performance, our models learn delay and slew infor-  
mation simultaneously.  
In this paper, the training data are built using a model-driven  
HW generator framework. Targeting a specific CMOS ASIC technology,  
component features and ground truth labels are extracted using open-  
source EDA tools. Fig. 1 shows the complete data collection flow.  
ML models using one-hot encoded and embedding feature vectors are  
trained and fine-tuned. The trained models are used to get estimated  
pin-to-pin delay values. To improve the performance of our models,  
slew information is included in the feature set. Moreover, we sequen-  
tially predict slew and delay values per component and then propagate  
the predicted slew along the delay path.  
In STA, all paths in a design are analyzed using gate delays and  
signal arrival times, especially the path with maximum and minimum  
delay. STA provides a severity measure called slack: a negative slack  
means that the signal through the path is too late, a positive slack  
means that the cells in the path could be replaced by ones with  
higher delay (and potentially, lower area) without causing timing  
violations [5].  
STA highly depends on the delay values used for each gate in the  
whole design. STA tools obtain the delay values using a Non-linear  
Delay Model (NLDM). Specifically, the delay of a cell is defined as  
a Lookup Table (LUT) with two variables as indexes: input slew and  
fanout load. The former is the time that a signal requires to make a  
transition. If the signal changes from low to high, it is called a rising  
edge. From high to low, it is called a falling edge. The fanout is the ca-  
pacitive load at the output [4]. For a constant output load, an increase  
in the input slew will reduce the delay. For a constant input transition,  
the delay will increase when the fanout capacitance increases. The slew  
is propagated along the circuit. The output slew of a cell is the input  
slew of the next connected cell in the timing path. Output slews are also  
defined via LUTs with input slew and fanout load as indexes. An NLDM  
stores delay and slew LUTs in a technology library as the Synopsis  
Liberty file [6]. STA tools can read those liberty files. During TA, the  
tool uses interpolation or extrapolation to calculate delays and output  
slews for index values that are not specified or outside the defined index  
tables. Moreover, STA tools are also able to scale the delay and slew  
values according to the current operating conditions, which physically  
affect the cell behavior such as temperature, voltage, and process [1].  
In this paper, an NLDM is employed for extracting ground truth  
labels for the delay and slew values. The prediction of these metrics  
is defined as a multi-objective regression problem that can be solved  
using ML.  
To evaluate the models’ performance, hardware adders are used  
as proof of concept. Specifically, three different architectures of 4-bit  
prefix adders with three different input transition times are compared.  
The trained models were used to predict all the pin-to-pin delays of all  
primitive components in the designs. When training a model with a  
dataset of logic gates and multiplexers, a simple Multilayer Perceptron  
(MLP) using one-hot encoding achieves, on average, a coefficient of  
determination ꢀ2 of 87% w.r.t delay values from the EDA tool. The  
MLP model using embedding feature vectors achieves an ꢀ2 score of  
79%. Training with an enlarged dataset, the best results are obtained  
by the MLP architecture using an embedding input layer. This approach  
reaches, on average, an ꢀ2 of 77% and a Mean Squared Error (MSE) of  
0.68%. Both models are around 8.4× faster than the EDA tools.  
The results show that generalizing to more component types de-  
creases the ꢀ2 score. Nevertheless, RTL designs are not solely composed  
of logic gates and multiplexers. Thus, using the enlarged dataset is  
considered the most viable approach. Compared to EDA tools, our  
approach represents a good trade-off between the quality of the pre-  
dictions and runtime.  
This paper is organized as follows: Section 2 briefly reviews con-  
cepts related to Timing Analysis (TA) and to ML applied to regression  
tasks. Section 3 provides an overview of the delay estimation state of  
the art. Section 4 describes our ML solution. Section 5 provides details  
of the benchmark circuits used to evaluate our trained models. The  
experiments and results comparing models trained on both datasets are  
summarized and discussed in Section 6. Finally, Section 7 concludes by  
mentioning open challenges, application fields, and future directions.  
2.2. ML for regression tasks  
In supervised learning, models are trained to learn the relationship  
between data features and ground-truth labels for regression or classifi-  
cation. In this paper, a model is trained to solve a regression task. The  
building blocks required to solve such a task using ML are:  
2
   
D. Sánchez L. et al.  
Microprocessors and Microsystems 94 (2022) 104671  
2.2.3. Model selection  
Once the training data and their features are selected, the archi-  
tecture of the ML model is defined so that the last layer outputs the  
predicted vector. In this paper, an MLP [13] is used due to its  
simplicity and good performance in multi-objective tasks. A simple  
MLP can represent a wide variety of functions when using appropriate  
parameters. Fig. 2(a) sketches the main layers of an MLP: input, hidden,  
and output layer. The input layer is made of as many neurons as the  
number of input features. For all our models, the number of hidden  
layers, as well as the number of nodes for those, are hyperparameters.  
The output layer has as many neurons as the number of objectives.  
The modules are fully connected, i.e., the value of each element of the  
subsequent layer is the value of the so-called activation function applied  
to the weighted sum of all the values of the current layer. The NN  
uses the backpropagation learning method to update all weights during  
training.  
Fig. 2. Implemented ML Models.  
The input features for the first layer should be numerical or one-hot  
encoded. Another alternative is to use embedding vectors [14]. Embed-  
ding vectors are widely used in Natural Language Processing (NLP) to  
embed words and to learn similarities between them [15,16]. Feature  
embeddings alleviate two main issues of one-hot-encoded vectors: high  
dimensionality and sparsity. Embedding layers are trained to learn the  
optimal mapping of a unique entry to a vector of a desired dimension.  
2.2.1. Data collection  
In a regression problem, the feature vectors ꢁꢂ per row ꢂ are labeled  
with the ꢃ target objectives ꢄꢃ. The goal is to train a model that, given  
an unseen feature vector ꢁ, predicts the quantitative values ꢄ.  
2.2.4. Hyperparameter search  
2.2.2. Feature analysis  
Hyperparameters are model parameters that cannot be trained. Us-  
ing optimal hyperparameters improves the model performance. Thus,  
hyperparameter search should be conducted. Manual tuning, grid  
search, and random search are considered brute-force methods. Grid  
and random search perform better than manual tuning, but they do  
not consider past evaluations. Hence, they lose significant time by  
evaluating hyperparameters in the same inadequate region [17]. There-  
fore, a model-based sequential method called Tree-Structured Parzen  
Estimator (TPE) was introduced [17]. TPE creates a surrogate model  
of the objective function and considers past evaluations to choose new  
hyperparameters. This work uses TPE to fine-tune all models.  
To construct meaningful datasets, statistical approaches for feature  
analysis should be used to analyze the correlation and importance of  
selected features:  
2.2.2.1 Spearman’s Rank Correlation Coefficient (SRCC): One method  
to analyze discrete and continuous variables is the correlation be-  
tween features. A special case of correlation is called the SRCC [7].  
It measures how well the relationship between two variables can be  
represented by a monotonic function, which may be linear or not. An  
SRCC of one indicates that two variables increase in value together.  
An SRCC of −1 corresponds to a decreasing monotonic trend: as one  
variable increases, the other variable decreases.  
2.2.5. Model evaluation  
The models using optimal hyperparameters are trained. During  
training, validation and testing, the performance of the model should  
be assessed. In a regression task, this performance can be measured  
using the Mean Absolute Error (MAE), MSE, and the coefficient of  
determination ꢀ2. The MAE in Eq. (1) is the average of the absolute  
difference between real and predicted values, where ꢄꢂ is the ground  
2.2.2.2. Mutual Information: The MI [8] between two variables ꢅ and  
ꢆ measures any dependency between them. A MI of zero indicates  
that the variables are independent. In other cases, the MI is a positive  
value, where higher values indicate higher dependency. To quantify the  
impact of ꢅ on the fluctuation of ꢆ , the MI is represented as the sum  
of the conditional entropy of ꢅ given ꢆ and ꢆ given ꢅ. The entropies  
are estimated using K-Nearest Neighbors (KNNs) [9] distances over the  
permutation of the features ꢅ. KNN is a non-parametric method that  
searches through the training set the k-closest instances to an unseen  
data point and returns the closest data as the prediction.  
truth and ꢄ̂ the predicted. MSE averages the squared value of that  
ꢂ
difference as shown in Eq. (2). MAE and MSE are suitable for datasets  
with outliers. The lower the values of MSE and MAE, the higher the  
prediction accuracy has the regression model. The ꢀ2 in Eq. (3) where  
ꢄ̄ is the mean value, measures how well the data fits the model.  
The higher the ꢀ2 value, the better the model is. The ꢀ2 can also  
be negative, indicating that the mean is a better estimator than the  
prediction.  
2.2.2.3. F-Test: Contrary to MI, F-Test [10] captures only the linear  
dependency between two random variables, i.e., estimates their degree  
of linear dependency. In linear regression tasks, F-Test is a number in  
the range of [0, 1] and measures how relevant independent variables  
are. To that end, the correlation between each feature in ꢅ and ꢆ is  
calculated. Then, the F-statistic is applied over the number of samples.

2.2.2.4. Shapley values: Shapley values [11] are a concept from game  
theory that is being used for explainable and interpretable Artificial  
Intelligence (AI) [12]. Shapley regression values measure feature im-  
portance for linear models, i.e., compute which features contribute  
more to a prediction. To that end, two different models are trained with  
and without a chosen feature, respectively. The performance of both  
models is compared, and the differences are computed for all possible  
feature subsets. The final Shapley values are the weighted average over  
all possible subsets [12].

3. Related work  
The NLDM is used by STA tools when the gate-level netlist is  
available. From a higher level of abstraction, other methods estimate  
the delay without using EDA tools:  
3
           
D. Sánchez L. et al.  
Microprocessors and Microsystems 94 (2022) 104671  
Table 1  
3.0.1. Analytical  
Selected features and objectives.  
Analytical methods attempt to relate delay to other features of the  
design.  
Feature/Objective  
Description  
Component type (ꢅ0)  
One of ꢏ a possible types  
The Logical Effort (LE) method [18] relates the delay of a CMOS  
circuit to the LE value, which is defined as the ratio of the input ca-  
pacitance of a gate, and the input capacitance of an inverter delivering  
the same output current. It is estimated as counting transistor widths  
for the gate divided by transistor widths for the inverter. The gate  
delay is defined as the sum of the effort delay and parasitic or zero-  
load delay. The effort delay is the LE multiplied by the fanout. The LE  
method allows comparing different implementations without running  
synthesis, it is technology-independent, and it can be used in multi-  
stage designs. However, it does not consider the effect of transition  
times of the inputs, and it uses estimations for the parasitic delay and  
LE values.  
Input number (ꢅ1)  
Current input pin considered  
Random number ∈ [1, ꢐ]b  
Number of inputs (ꢅ2)  
Bit width inputs (ꢅ3)  
Random number ∈ [1, 32]  
Bit width output (ꢅ4)  
Connections to input (ꢅ5)  
Connections to output (ꢅ6)  
Slew In [ꢑꢒꢓꢓ, ꢔꢂꢕꢖ] (ꢅ7)  
Number of outputsc (ꢅ8)  
Sum bit width inputsc (ꢅ9)  
Avg. bit width inputsc (ꢅ10)  
Random number ∈ [1, 32]  
Connected components ∈ [0, 6]  
Connected components ∈ [0, 6]  
Transition times for current pin  
Random number ∈ [1, 32]  
Sum over all inputs bit width  
Average over all inputs bit width

Delay from current to output pin

bIf training set is DS-I, then ꢐ = 12. Otherwise, ꢐ = 32.  
cExtra features considered for DS-II.  
In [19], four analytical novel methods are introduced to estimate  
delay given the placement of a circuit. Using the same technology and  
same router, base model designs are used to extract factors such as net  
degree, route length, net delay, and route Steiner tree length. Those  
factors are terms of the estimation functions, which can be quadratic  
or linear equations. For instance, the proposed Delay Sampling method  
computes delay as a quadratic function of the pin-to-pin placement  
length. With base designs, the coefficients are computed, and for new  
designs, the equation is used with the current wire’s length. This  
method uses scaling factors and LUTs to link the estimated variables  
per net. Thus, it can be slow and computationally expensive for larger  
designs.  
initial parameter extraction for the best-fit polynomial uses few test  
designs.  
In [25], a one-shot scheme predicts the area-delay curve for Boolean  
functions. This method proposes a novel delay complexity measure  
based on the on and off-sets of a Boolean function. According to the  
authors, the size and the number of prime implicants of a function  
affect the time needed for evaluating the function. An STA tool is  
used to generate a family of curves of delay values versus the novel  
complexity measure. This method provides a minimum and maximum  
delay estimation. However, it does not support circuits with XOR and  
other complex gates.  
In [20], the delay is estimated as a logarithmic function of the  
complexity of the gate and the fanout. Type of gate and fanout values  
are extracted from the designs before logic synthesis. These are scaled  
by constant coefficients or technology-dependent factors calculated  
using the NOT and NAND delay, the fanout limit, and the standard  
load. In this paper, the authors did not consider the effects of the signal  
transition times on the estimation.  
3.0.4. Machine learning-based  
In [26], an ML-based delay estimator is used during FPGA place-  
ment. They use ML for three main reasons. First, training, validation,  
and inference are faster than running traditional EDA tools. Delays are  
non-linear functions in which ML has proved high performance. Finally,  
an ML-based estimator can also be easily updated. They build a dataset  
with 20 features, where the most relevant are: number of pins, Half  
Perimeter Wire Length (HPWL), and average congestion.  
In [21], the delay is approximated as a linear function of the number  
of programmable interconnect points and the number of segments  
between two circuit points. Scaling coefficients were computed from  
observations done using the Xilinx timing analysis tool. The approxi-  
mation function estimates delay for Field Programmable Gate Arrays  
(FPGAs) considering also the wiring effects, but it does not support  
ASIC.  
Our paper is comparable to [26], as we defined the delay estimation  
as a regression problem. However, we estimate the delay at RTL and  
use delay training data after logic synthesis rather than after placement  
to avoid learning inefficient mappings done during physical synthe-  
sis. [26] targets FPGA, not ASIC technology, therefore they did not  
consider any effect of slew. [26] uses Random Forest (RF), and we  
use NNs, a more expensive method that can generalize better than RF  
when using appropriate parameters. To further improve, we perform  
a hyperparameter search. Compared to other methods, our approach  
can be used for all types of logic gates and more complex components.  
Moreover, the modeling part is easier, as only the dataset has to be  
generated rather than modeling the delay gate behavior with statistical  
or mathematical functions.  
3.0.2. Macromodeling  
Macromodeling is a usual way to estimate design metrics from a  
high-level circuit description. It can be seen as a template, in which the  
quantity under analysis is related to a set of parameters and coefficients.  
The set of parameters is computed by using a set of base designs.  
Commonly, linear regression is used to define the actual equation of  
the model.  
In [22], a macromodeling technique is used to estimate the delay  
at the behavioral level. Technology-independent gate-level netlists are  
mapped to Binary Decision Diagrams (BDDs). For delay estimation, the  
model relates various types of the complexity of a Boolean function to  
its gate-level implementation as size, number of inputs, and density.  
In [23], a macromodel for delay estimation at RTL is proposed as  
a function of ꢌꢍꢍ and ꢌꢎ. The two scaling coefficients are extracted  
from experiments in HSPICE. However, the model only supports NAND,  
NOR, and NOT gates.  
4. Method  
Gate delay estimation is challenging due to the many dependencies:  
technology, operating conditions, cell types, outputs capacitance, and  
signal transition times. Therefore, we proposed a new approach to  
estimate cell delays using NNs. We define delay pin-to-pin estimation  
as a multi-objective regression problem. To get a more accurate delay  
estimation, the slew per gate is also predicted and propagated. The  
input for the regression model is a feature vector ꢀꢁ where ꢂ is a pin-to-  
pin connection of a component within a design. The vector ꢀ encodes  
information of the features from Table 1. The output of the model ꢂꢁ  
is a vector of four components representing the regression objectives:  
[ꢕꢓꢖꢗ_ꢘꢙꢎ_ꢑꢒꢓꢓ, ꢕꢓꢖꢗ_ꢘꢙꢎ_ꢔꢂꢕꢖ, ꢍꢖꢓꢒꢄ_ꢑꢒꢓꢓ, ꢍꢖꢓꢒꢄ_ꢔꢂꢕꢖ]  
3.0.3. Polynomial-based  
Polynomial models are regression-based, where the delay is the de-  
pendent variable, and design parameters are the independent variables.  
QUEST [24] is a tool that uses best-fit polynomial estimation for  
area and delay from RTL descriptions. QUEST transforms the RTL code  
to a technology-independent form, similar to what a logic synthesis tool  
does. But it only targets three logic gates: AND, OR, and NOT. The

4.1. Data collection  
Fig. 1 sketches the whole flow for dataset generation. Our proposed  
method is independent of any RTL implementation. However, we use  
a HW generator framework, which generates our designs in a faster  
and automated way by using meta-modeling. The used HW generation  
framework is MetaRTL [27]. It applies the Model Driven Architecture  
(MDA) principle to RTL generation [28]. The abstract model (Com-  
putation Independent Model (CIM) in MDA) hierarchically describes  
configuration options.  
For both datasets, the generated Verilog code is synthesized using  
Yosys,1 [29] an open-source tool that reads Verilog Hardware Descrip-  
tion Language (HDL) files and creates a gate-level netlist by mapping it  
to CMOS cells of the technology library. We use a 40 nm technology for  
our experiments. The synthesized netlists are the required input to the  
STA. In this work, OpenSTA2 [30] is used, an open-source version of  
the commercial Parallax timer tool. A technology library configuration  
is chosen, and the tool is set up with parameters exemplarily shown in  
Table 3.  
4.2. Feature selection  
In this paper, different features are selected from two different  
datasets. We generate random configurations of components to build  
two different training sets: A simple dataset (DS-I) and a generalized  
dataset (DS-II).  
To consider variations of the slew, we generate timing reports with  
two and four different variants for the transition time of the input  
ports for the datasets DS-I and DS-II, respectively. The timing report  
style is configured using TCL scripts. Pin-to-pin timing reports are  
collected for all components within a design. To that end, the tool is  
configured to analyze all the four possible timing paths i.e. Register-  
to-Register, Register-to-Output, Register-to-Input and Input-to-Output, and  
the maximum edges: max rise and max fall. Automatically, we read the  
generated reports and extract information related to arc type, slew, and  
delay. These are used to find the maximum rise and fall delay and the  
corresponding slew values.  
4.2.1. DS-I  
In this dataset, twenty types of RTL components are used to build  
the training designs: multiplexers, inverters, and logic gates (AND,  
NAND, OR, NOR, XOR, and XNOR). The latter in a bitwise, logical,  
and reduced fashion. In DS-I, each design contains only the above-  
mentioned RTL components, with a randomly generated number of  
inputs and bit widths. The number of inputs varies from one up to  
12. Input bit widths range from one to 32. To consider different fan  
outs, the number of components per design ranges from zero up to  
six. Single-component designs have zero connections to the output pin.  
Additionally, two different transition times of primary input ports are  
considered during the TA. Our collected dataset DS-I consists of 42.9ꢚ  
samples.  
RTL features and information from EDA tools are mapped to a  
tabular dataset. Each row of the dataset or sample represents a pin-to-  
pin connection of an RTL component inside a circuit. Table 2 shows the  
number of samples per component category. The columns of the dataset  
contain the RTL features as well as ground truth labels. The generation  
of our dataset following the steps shown in Fig. 1 took around three  
weeks when running in a CPU machine.  
Table 1 lists our feature set ꢅ and objectives ꢆ . In this subsection,  
the motivation of our selection is presented:  
4.2.2. DS-II  
DS-I is extended to cover other component types such as demul-  
tiplexing, arithmetic, and branch operators. This enlarged dataset is  
what we name DS-II. Table 2 lists all 37 types of primitive components  
covered. Additionally, input bit widths range from one to 32 for one up  
to 32 inputs.  
(1) ꢅ0: It gives an intuition about the gate’s complexity. It also cor-  
relates to implementation patterns (e.g., the transistor netlist).  
(2) ꢅ1: To distinguish the pin-to-pin connection from the current  
pin to the output. It reflects the signal’s path through the design,  
especially if inputs have a different function (e.g., multiplexer).  
Different component types have different ranges for the selected  
RTL features. For instance, a branch operator component such as GT  
has two inputs and one output per definition, while for logic gates,  
the number of inputs is at least one. Thus, in the generated random  
configurations, the number of inputs for GT is fixed to two, while for  
logic gates is in the range of [1, 32]. This results in an imbalanced  
1
2

https://github.com/The-OpenROAD-Project/OpenSTA.  
5
           
D. Sánchez L. et al.  
Microprocessors and Microsystems 94 (2022) 104671  
Fig. 3. Spearman’s Correlation (SRCC) Matrix.  
(3) ꢅ2, ꢅ3, and ꢅ4: The time needed to execute a function depends  
on the number of inputs and their sizes. The number of inputs  
correlates to the implementation pattern.  
4.3.2. MI and F-test:  
Fig. 4 shows the MI (gray bars) and F-test statistic (orange bars)  
between the selected features. Fig. 4(a) shows the highest degree of  
linear dependency measured by the F-test is between the output slew  
and the features input slew ꢅ7 and fanout ꢅ6, while the MI is the  
highest for features related to input bit widths ꢅ9 and ꢅ10. Similarly,  
the MI between delay and ꢅ9 and ꢅ10 are the most significant, as shown  
in Fig. 4(b). Moreover, the F-test indicates that the most relevant linear  
dependency occurs between the delay and the output bit width ꢅ4.  
(4) ꢅ5 and ꢅ6: Gate delay depends on the fanout, which is propor-  
tional to the number of connections.  
(5) ꢅ7: Gate’s output slew is dependent on the slew model, which  
is also a function of the input slew and the fanout. The slew for  
primary ports is configured in the STA tool. The slew for other  
connections is propagated.  
(6) ꢅ8: The number of outputs correlates with the component im-  
plementation. It should be considered for multiple output com-  
ponents (e.g., demultiplexers).  
4.3.3. Shapley values:  
The Shapley values highlight the impact of each selected feature in  
the prediction of each objective. To that end, an XGBoost [31] regressor  
model is used as an explainer. Fig. 5(a) shows that the most significant  
features for the output slew prediction were related to the fanout ꢅ6  
and the input slew ꢅ7ꢑ as expected. For the delay prediction, RTL  
features such as the bit width of the output port ꢅ4 and the number  
of inputs ꢅ2 have higher Shapley values, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The  
input slew is again among the top five most important features.  
(7) ꢅ9, ꢅ10: The bit width of other input pins different from the one  
indicated with ꢅ1 gives information about the complexity of the  
logic mapping.  
In both datasets, the data types of the selected features are mostly  
integers or floating-point values. For the component type ꢅ0, the data  
type is a string with the name of one of the component types supported.  
4.3. Feature analysis  
4.4. Neural networks  
This paper uses the SRCC over both datasets. Moreover, we calculate  
the MI, F-test, and Shapley Values for DS-II, specifically for the features  
ꢅ and the two objectives: output slew (ꢆ1) and delay (ꢆ2) distinguished  
into rising (r) and falling edge (f ).  
4.4.1. Pre-processing  
In both datasets DS-I and DS-II, each sample is a pin-to-pin con-  
nection of a randomly generated design using different variants for the  
input transition times.  
For simplicity, only the results for the falling edge, i.e., ꢆ1ꢑ and  
ꢆ2ꢑ are shown. The results for the rising edge highlight similar trends  
described below. The only difference is that the dependence between  
the input slew feature for the rising edge ꢅ7ꢔ and objectives with same  
For both datasets, the categorical feature ꢅ0 is one-hot encoded.  
ꢅ0 is mapped to an ꢊ × ꢏ sparse matrix, where ꢊ is the number of  
samples and ꢏ the number of component types supported, i.e., 20 for  
DS-I, and 37 for DS-II. Additionally, we map the bit widths per input  
to a zero-padded vector, resulting in an ꢊ × ꢐ matrix, where ꢐ = 12  
and ꢐ = 32 for DS-I and DS-II, respectively. A MinMaxScaler is used to  
scale the four objectives in the range [0, 1].  
edge type, i.e., ꢆ1ꢔ and ꢆ2ꢔ, is stronger than with the falling edge ꢅ7ꢑ  
.
In the following feature analysis, the categorical feature ꢅ0 was not  
included.  
Both datasets were split into training (60%), validation (30%), and  
testing (10%) sets.  
4.3.1. SRCC:  
Fig. 3 shows the SRCC matrix pairwise calculated for both datasets.  
For DS-I, all the features ꢅꢂ correlate with the objectives ꢆ2. As ex-  
pected, there is a high correlation between ꢆ2 and ꢅ7 features and with  
structure-related features such as ꢅ1, ꢅ2, and ꢅ3. Similarly, the features  
ꢅꢂ correlate with the objectives ꢆ2 for DS-II. As expected, there is a high  
correlation between ꢆ1 and ꢅ6 features. The lower correlation between  
ꢅ5 with other features and objectives is because only one component  
type of the dataset has multiple outputs. For the remaining component  
types, the number of outputs is fixed to one.  
4.4.2. Architectures  
This paper compares two main architectures: a simple MLP and  
the TabMLP architecture from [32]. The simple MLP architecture is  
sketched in Fig. 2(a). Each hidden layer uses a ReLU activation function  
with a dropout layer to avoid overfitting. Training the MLP model with  
DS-I, the number of input neurons is 41 and 43 with and without input  
slew features, respectively. Input slew features are always considered  
6
 
D. Sánchez L. et al.  
Microprocessors and Microsystems 94 (2022) 104671  
Fig. 4. MI (bars colored in gray) and F-Test (bars colored in orange) values for falling edge in DS-II. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader  
is referred to the web version of this article.)  
Fig. 5. Shapley values for falling edge in DS-II.  
model. For DS-I, ꢟ is equal to 10. The total number of numerical  
input features is 21 and 23, with and without considering the slew,  
respectively. The number of neurons in the output layer is two when  
predicting rise and fall delay or four when including the prediction  
of output slew for the same edges. For DS-II, ꢟ is 19. Due to the  
new supported ranges and the consideration of slew features, the total  
number of numerical input features is 43, and the output layer has four  
neurons.  
By using the two mentioned ML model architectures, we build six  
models: For DS-I, we train four models: an MLP, and TabMLP, with and  
without considering slew. For DS-II, we employ the same architectures  
but always consider slews. Using the TPE implementation from [33],  
the best set of hyperparameters for each model is found and shown in  
Table 4.  
4.4.3. Training, validating and testing  
The models were trained in a Nvidia Tesla P40 GPU for 300 epochs.  
For the MLP model, the overall training time when using DS-I was  
164.9 s, and for the TabMLP model 706.5 s. For DS-II, the training times  
were 3.7 h and 4.4 h for the MLP and TabMLP models, respectively.  
Fig. 6 shows the results when evaluating each model over the test  
dataset. The test dataset consists of generated designs using one gate  
with zero, up to six connected gates. As shown, the models achieve an  
accuracy higher than 86% even without considering slew features nor  
propagation.  
Fig. 6. Avg. results of estimation in test set for DS-I and DS-II.  
when training with DS-II. As the supported component types and the  
maximum number of inputs increased, the input layer of the MLP model  
has 80 neurons.  
The TabMLP architecture in Fig. 2(b) uses an embedding layer  
encoding the categorical feature ꢅ0 into a ꢟ-dimensional vector, which  
is concatenated with the other features and passed through an MLP

directly connected to them, the input slew is considered as the pre-  
dicted output slew of the connected output. To consider slew propa-  
gation, the algorithm predicts the output slew and delay sequentially,  
starting from the closest nodes to the input ports. Finally, our es-  
timations are compared with results from OpenSTA using the same  
configuration parameters.

6.1. Results for dataset DS-I  
8
13  
In the following, the performance of the models MLP and TabMLP  
is evaluated when using DS-I as the training dataset. In particular, this  
experiment analyzes the effect of learning slew values simultaneously  
and propagating them to the next connected component in the timing  
path.  
5. Application  
Adders are a key component in ASIC designs. The simplest version  
of an adder is the Ripple Carry Adder (RCA). An ꢊ-bit RCA is built  
using ꢊ full adders, the carry-out bit of the previous stage becomes  
the carry-in of the current stage. At any particular time, only one  
full adder instance is used [34]. More complex architectural versions  
are the Parallel Prefix adders, as they split the computation into three  
stages: Pre-processing, Carry-propagation, and Post-processing [35].  
Three main architectures of parallel prefix adders are shown in Fig. 7:  
SKA, BKA, and KSA. In the gray-colored stage, rectangles calculate  
the propagate and generate bits for primary inputs. In the blue stage,  
generate and propagate values per bit are calculated using: generate  
blocks (circles), generate and propagate blocks (dark circles), and  
buffers (triangles). In the final stage, total sum and carry bits are  
computed [35]. In general, KSA occupies more area and is faster than  
a BKA. SKA has the same delay as KSA, but it occupies a lesser area for  
a higher number of bits.  
Table 6 compares our estimated delay values w.r.t the results from  
the STA tool, when using the MLP and the TabMLP architectures.  
Table 6 shows the ꢀ2 value for fall and rise delay estimation. The  
MLP without slew information provides a low estimation accuracy.  
To increase the accuracy, we add slew features to the dataset. We  
incorporate slew estimation and propagation in our final method, as  
the input slew is known only for the input ports. The second last row  
of Table 6 confirms the strategy and shows the high ꢀ2 values. The  
same experiments were conducted using TabMLP.  
To evaluate the stability of the models across different input slew  
configurations, our models are run for both known configuration vari-  
ants (V1 and V2) and one unknown but similar (V3). Table 7 shows  
that for V1 and V2, the results have a ꢀ2 score higher than 80% for all  
adders and both models. For V3, the ꢀ2 decreases as unknown input  
slews give a wrong predicted output slew, which will be propagated  
and affects the overall prediction of the delay. However, the MAE and  
MSE are still low. For simplicity, the results for rising and falling edges  
are averaged.  
We implement the three architectures for 4-bit with one carry input  
and carry output bit. The three implementations have the same number  
of primary ports and bit widths. They also use three types of primitives:  
Bitwise XOR, AND, and OR. The maximum number of inputs per gate  
is two. Table 5 compares other details of the RTL implementation.  
The MLP model achieves on average 6.76% MAE, 0.96% MSE, and  
ꢀ2 equal to 0.87. The experiments were repeated using the TabMLP  
model. Table 7 shows that this model achieves on average 9.49% MAE,  
1.39% MSE, and ꢀ2 equal to 0.79.  
6. Results and discussion  
6.2. Results for dataset DS-II  
All the experiments were executed in a Linux CPU Intel Xeon E5-  
2630 v4 at 2.20 GHz. This section summarizes the results of different  
experiments:  
Based on the results for DS-I, we consider learning and propagating  
slew values as our final approach for delay prediction. We extend  
Table 6 with the ꢀ2 values of MLP and TabMLP when the training  
set is DS-II. The results show how the TabMLP model could generalize  
slightly better to more RTL primitives than the MLP model.

To evaluate the stability of the models across different input slew  
configurations, we run our models for the configurations V1, V2, and  
V3, which were also covered in the training set. Table 8 shows that  
the results for different variants are similar in all circuits and models.  
The MLP model achieves, on average, 7.94% MAE, 0.99% MSE, and  
ꢀ2 equal to 0.67. The TabMLP outperforms MLP with a 6.91% MAE,  
0.68% MSE and ꢀ2 score of 0.77 on average over the 4-bit adders.  
Fig. 8. Runtime comparison between ML models and EDA tools.  
6.4. Results for larger adders  
To evaluate the performance of the models for larger designs, we  
predict delays of RTL components inside 8, 16, 32, and 64-bit adders  
for the different STA variants.  
6.3. Comparison DS-I and DS-II  
When comparing the results for 4-bit and larger adders, the ꢀ2 score  
drops from 0.77 to 0.73 for TabMLP and from 0.67 to 0.59 for MLP.  
For both models and larger adders, the average MSE was around 1%  
and a MAE around 7%. This shows that the quality of the models is  
slightly reduced by increasing design complexity.  
In the following, the models are compared in terms of runtime and  
performance when training with both datasets.  
6.3.1. Performance  
Fig. 8 shows the average inference time and the time required to  
synthesize and run the STA tool for the same designs. On average, any  
ML model is 8.4× faster. For the 64-bit adders, the time benefit is more  
evident, as the ML models are faster by 11.5×.  
Table 7 shows the average results for both models when trained over  
DS-I. The averaged ꢀ2 coefficient for MLP is 0.87 and for TabMLP, 0.79.  
Thus, the averaged absolute difference for the ꢀ2 coefficient between  
both models is 0.08. In this case, the best architecture is MLP. When  
training with DS-II, the averaged ꢀ2 coefficients shown in Table 8  
were 0.67 and 0.77 for MLP and TabMLP, respectively. Thus, TabMLP  
outperforms MLP by 0.1.  
6.5. Comparison with baseline methods  
We compare our approach with the related works in Section 3: The  
modeling effort of NNs is high compared to analytical, polynomial,  
or macromodeling approaches due to the effort for collecting our  
significant dataset. Other approaches used none or fewer samples to  
approximate coefficients. In terms of runtime, this is lower thanks to  
the use of GPUs for training and inference. The gain in execution time  
is the main advantage of ML over other approaches. Ours and methods  
such as [21,25,26] need a delay characterization that uses STA tools  
to extract a ground truth value for the delay. This costs execution  
time and licenses if not open source tools are used as in our case.  
Analytical methods such as [18] overcome this. However, our approach  
is highly accurate on the test set and accurate on real designs. Finally,  
our approach can be extended to any RTL component, and it considers  
input signal transition times.  
6.3.2. Runtime  
For both datasets, the TabMLP runtime is around 9 s. TabMLP is  
faster as it encodes the component type in a lower ꢟ-dimensional  
vector. On the contrary, the MLP model uses a one-hot encoded sparse  
matrix. Thus, the inference time increases from 10.98 s to 16.93 s for  
DS-I and DS-II, respectively  
In general, the quality of the predictions for the DS-II decreased  
compared to the results for DS-I. This is due to three factors:  
(1) The more component types supported, the sparser the feature  
matrix is.  
Similar to [26], this paper evaluates the performance of shallow  
methods for estimating pin-to-pin delays. Table 9 shows the hyperpa-  
rameters for the two regressors: Decision Trees (DTs) and an RF. We  
train both models with DS-II. Table 8 shows the prediction results when  
running inference over the 4-bit adders. The deep models outperform  
the shallow regressors in all evaluation metrics and circuits. However,  
the runtimes of the four models are similar, as shown in Fig. 8.  
(2) The range of RTL features and their contribution to the predic-  
tion depends on the component type. For instance, a multiplexer  
has more than one input, while a NOT has only one. For the  
latter, the number of inputs is not a relevant feature and may  
disturb the learning process.  
(3) The component type also impacts the slew and delay ranges.  
Thus, a global scaling may destroy some patterns of the objec-  
tives. For instance, logic RTL components are represented with  
logic cells of the technology, while arithmetic components are  
mapped to a set of logic gates implementing that functionality.  
The delay and slew values are lower for logic components, than  
for arithmetic operators.  
7. Conclusion  
To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first approach  
analyzing the delay estimation in the early stages of the design process  
and mapping it to a multi-objective regression task solved by NNs.  
We built our dataset from generated RTL and learned slew and delay

[1] Synopsys timing constraints and optimization user guide, 2010.  
[2] G. Huang, J. Hu, Y. He, J. Liu, M. Ma, et al., Machine learning for electronic  
design automation: A survey, 26, Association for Computing Machinery, New  
York, NY, USA, 2021, no. 5.  
simultaneously to improve the performance of our models. We use two  
known architectures: a simple MLP and TabMLP, an MLP using an  
embedding layer for categorical variables. We train our models with  
two datasets: DS-I, which contains only logical component types, and  
DS-II, which supports 17 component types more.  
[3] D. Sánchez Lopera, L. Servadei, V.P. Kasi, S. Prebeck, W. Ecker, RTL delay  
prediction using neural networks, in: 2021 IEEE Nordic Circuits and Systems  
Conference (NorCAS), 2021, pp. 1–7.  
[4] IEEE standard for integrated circuit (IC) delay and power calculation system,  
IEEE Std 1481-1999, 2000.  
[5] R.B. Hitchcock, G.L. Smith, D.D. Cheng, Timing Analysis of Computer Hardware,  
IBM J. Res. Dev. 26 (1) (1982) 100–105.  
When training with DS-I, the MLP model is the best architecture.  
It achieves an average ꢀ2 of 87% taking 10.98 s. The TabMLP model is  
faster with 8.98 s but has a lower ꢀ2 of 79%. The results are the opposite  
when generalizing to more component types. Using the dataset DS-II,  
the TabMLP represents the best architecture. It achieves an average ꢀ2  
of 77% while keeping the inference time around 9 s. In summary,  
the quality of the predictions decreases with the generalization to  
more component types and the increasing complexity of the evaluation  
circuits. However, DS-II allows us to evaluate RTL designs of real  
complexity. The gain of our approach using ML increases proportionally  
with the number of components in the design, as this slows down the  
synthesis and STA tools but keeps estimation times low.  
[6] Liberty user guides and reference manual suite, 2013.  
[7] J.H. Zar, Spearman rank correlation, in: Encyclopedia of Biostatistics, Vol. 7,  
Wiley Online Library, 2005.  
[8] A. Kraskov, H. Stögbauer, P. Grassberger, Estimating mutual information, Phys.  
Rev. E (3) 69 (6) (2004) 66138.  
[9] E. Fix, J.L. Hodges, Discriminatory analysis. Nonparametric discrimination:  
Consistency properties, Internat. Statist. Rev. 57 (3) (1989) 238–247.  
[10] G.W. Snedecor, W.G. Cochran, Statistical Methods, Vol. 1191, eight ed., Iowa  
State University Press, Ames, Iowa, 1991, p. 524.  
[11] L.S. Shapley, A value for N-person games, RAND Corporation, Santa Monica, CA,  
1952.  
[12] S.M. Lundberg, S.-I. Lee, A unified approach to interpreting model predictions,  
in: Proceedings of the 31st International Conference on Neural Information  
Processing Systems (NIPS’17), Curran Associates Inc., Red Hook, NY, USA, 2017,  
pp. 4768–4777.  
In future work, we recognize four main directions. In the first place,  
we expect to find new ML architectures that can generalize better to  
different component types and circuit complexity. Second, our datasets  
can be extended to consider physical design information. Moreover,  
our solution is accurate when the transition times, technology library,  
and operation conditions used during training are kept constant during  
inference. Otherwise, new datasets have to be generated and models  
have to be retrained. We think that other ML techniques, such as trans-  
fer learning, can help to solve these challenges. Finally, the estimated  
delays can be accumulated to find critical and non-critical parts of an  
RTL design.  
[13] S.K. Pal, S. Mitra, Multilayer perceptron, fuzzy sets, classification, Trans. Neural  
Netw. 3 (5) (1992) 683–697.  
[14] X. Huang, A. Khetan, M. Cvitkovic, Z. Karnin, TabTransformer: Tabular data  
modeling using contextual embeddings, 2020, ArXiv, [Online]. Available: https:  
//arxiv.org/abs/2012.06678.  
[15] X. Rong, Word2vec parameter learning explained, 2014, ArXiv, [Online].  
Available: https://arxiv.org/abs/1411.2738.  
[16] J. Devlin, M. Chang, K. Lee, K. Toutanova, BERT: Pre-training of deep bidi-  
rectional transformers for language understanding, in: Proceedings of the 2019  
Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational  
Linguistics: Human Language Technologies NAACL-HLT, Vol. 1, Association for  
Computational Linguistics, 2019, pp. 4171–4186.

D. Sánchez L. et al.  
Microprocessors and Microsystems 94 (2022) 104671  
Daniela Sánchez Lopera received her B.Sc. degree in Elec-  
tronic Engineering at the University of Antioquia, Colombia  
in 2016. In 2020, she received her M.Sc. degree in Electrical  
and Computer Engineering with focus on Embedded and  
Computer Systems at the Technical University of Munich.  
Daniela Sánchez Lopera is currently a doctoral student at  
the Chair of Electronic Design Automation at the Technical  
University of Munich and at Infineon Technologies AG in  
Germany. Her main research interest is the application of  
Machine Learning to the field of Digital Design Automation.

independent logic equations, in: 1990 IEEE International Conference on  
Computer-Aided Design, IEEE, 1990.

in: Proceedings ED&TC European Design and Test Conference, IEEE, 1996, pp.  
151–157.

ests are methods of Reinforcement Learning applied to

Sebastian Prebeck is an AI Hardware Accelerator Design  
Senior Engineer. He started his career with the Bachelor’s  
degree (B.Sc.) in Electrical and Computer engineering at the  
Technical University of Munich (TUM). After graduation,  
he continued the education with the consecutive Master’s  
degree (M.Sc.) at TUM. Thereby, the focus was on digital  
design and power engineering. Currently, Mr. Prebeck par-  
ticipates in a doctoral program at TUM. His research domain  
is on low power AI Hardware. The research is done in close  
cooperation with the company Infineon Technologies AG in  
Munich.

2006.

Wolfgang Ecker is Distinguished Engineer at Infineon and  
Adjunct Professor at Technical University of Munich. He  
is author of over 200 papers on modeling and design  
automation, received 5 best paper awards, and was granted  
with the German EDA achievement award. Wolfgang is  
member of Acatech, the German Academy of Science and  
Engineering and has been member of the AI commission  
of inquiry of the German Parliament. Wolfgang Ecker also  
coordinates the Infineon Deep Learning internal think tank.
